title: Insalata di rinforzo
subtitle: Neapolitan cauliflower salad with olives, capers, and anchovies
excerpt: Blanched cauliflower dressed with olives, capers, anchovies, and red wine vinegar — the traditional Neapolitan Christmas salad. The name means reinforcement salad, because more ingredients are added each day to reinforce it as it is eaten down. Sharp, salty, and exactly right alongside rich festive food.
sourceNotes: Insalata di rinforzo is documented as the canonical Neapolitan Christmas Eve salad, served as part of the Feast of the Seven Fishes (La Vigilia) that precedes Christmas Day. Artusi includes Neapolitan vegetable preparations in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain); the salad is documented in Neapolitan household and regional cookbooks from the nineteenth century. The name refers to the tradition of adding more olives, capers, and pickled vegetables to the bowl each day as the salad diminishes.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better made at least 1 hour ahead; improves significantly over 24 hours in the fridge as the cauliflower absorbs the dressing.

content.text: Insalata di rinforzo is the salad that appears at every Neapolitan Christmas table as counterweight to the richness of everything else. The cauliflower is blanched just enough to lose its rawness without becoming soft; the dressing is aggressive with vinegar and salt from the anchovies, capers, and olives. It cuts through heavier food in the way that acid always does, and it gets better as it sits.

The name (reinforcement salad) comes from the tradition of replenishing the bowl each day as it is eaten. Families begin the salad on Christmas Eve and keep adding to it through to the 26th, each addition reinforcing the bowl with fresh olives, more capers, new pickled vegetables. It is, in effect, a living salad. For non-Christmas purposes, make it once and eat it over two days — it holds well.

items.prepNote: large, about 1 kg; broken into small florets
pitted; mixed black and green if preferred
rinsed; use small capers if possible
taste before adding; anchovies and capers are salty

content.text: Bring a large pan of generously salted water to a boil. Add the {{cauliflower}} florets and blanch for 6 to 8 minutes until just tender when pierced with a knife — not soft, still with a faint resistance. Drain and spread on a tray to cool and dry completely.

Transfer the cooled cauliflower to a large bowl. Add the {{olives-black}} of olives, the {{capers}} of capers, and the {{anchovies}} anchovy fillets. Pour over the {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar and the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil. Toss gently. Taste before seasoning — the anchovies and capers are already very salty; you may need no added salt. Add a generous amount of black pepper. Cover and leave at room temperature for at least 1 hour, or refrigerate overnight.

items.symptom: Cauliflower is mushy and falling apart
items.cause: Blanched too long; check from 5 minutes onwards
items.fix: Cauliflower for this salad should be just tender, not soft — it will soften further as it sits in the dressing

items.cause: Salt added without tasting; capers and anchovies not rinsed
items.fix: Rinse the capers; drain the anchovies well; taste before adding any extra salt

items.symptom: Dressing does not penetrate the cauliflower
items.cause: Not rested long enough; cauliflower added while still warm
items.fix: Cool the cauliflower completely before dressing; rest for at least 1 hour, preferably overnight

items.symptom: Salad tastes flat despite all the salty components
items.cause: Not enough vinegar; or red wine vinegar too mild
items.fix: This salad should taste aggressively acidic; taste and add more vinegar if needed; the acid softens over time

content.text: Insalata di rinforzo is a dish that exists on Christmas Eve tables across Naples and Campania — almost without exception, almost without variation. The Feast of the Seven Fishes is a southern Italian Catholic tradition that prohibits meat on Christmas Eve and fills the table with fish, shellfish, and vegetable preparations instead. Insalata di rinforzo is the salad component: sharp, briny, vegetable-led, doing exactly what a salad should do alongside rich baccalà fritto and anguilla in umido. Outside its Neapolitan context it is a very good cauliflower salad that works alongside any rich protein: a demonstration of how much flavour a handful of pantry ingredients can deliver when they are the right pantry ingredients.
